summary: 2014 winners and group favourites under Julian Nagelsmann, but desperate to avoid a third straight group-stage exit. A clear possession-and-press identity — 'more style than substance' so far (Euro 2024 QF, beaten in the 2025 Nations League finals). Five straight qualifying wins after an opening defeat in Slovakia.
style: Flexible — a preferred 4-2-3-1 (likely vs the group's lesser sides), occasionally 3-4-3. Wide duo Raum and Kimmich (Kimmich kept deeper in build-up, attack-minded Raum pushed on). Want possession and territory, press aggressively, and now attack more vertically (post-Kroos/Gündoğan) with a fluid, roaming front four.
strengths: Control and territorial domination; aggressive pressing to suffocate opponents
An abundance of attacking-midfield/winger quality with freedom to roam
~31% of qualifying goals from set plays
weaknesses: Centre-backs have struggled to defend touch-tight — better teams have got in behind
No settled first-choice No 9; Serge Gnabry ruled out injured

keyPlayers.name: Florian Wirtz
keyPlayers.note: Nagelsmann's leading attacker (10 goals, 8 assists in 29 caps); the magic to break down compact blocks — after a settling-in season at Liverpool

keyPlayers.name: Jamal Musiala
keyPlayers.note: 7 goals, 5 assists since Sep 2023; back from a leg break — the other No 10 to unlock low blocks

keyPlayers.name: Kai Havertz
keyPlayers.note: Nagelsmann's likely No 9 if fit (with Woltemade and the in-form Undav as alternatives)

tactics: Dominate the ball, press high, and rely on the No 10s' magic to break compact blocks — all three group opponents will sit deep. The open question is the front-four selection and the No 9. Touch-tight central defending is the vulnerability against teams that can play through.
notes: Chasing a first deep run since the 2014 title; exited the group in both 2018 and 2022.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group E (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
